title: The figure-8 knot
subtitle: A stopper knot for securing cord ends and preventing fraying
excerpt: The figure-8 knot is a stopper knot: its role is to prevent a cord end from passing through a hole, a ring, or back through a knot group. In macramé, it secures hanging cord ends below a fringe, prevents plant-hanger cord from slipping through a ceramic pot hole, and forms a neat end-knot at the base of a piece where a bulkier knot would sit awkwardly.
sourceNotes: The figure-8 knot is a standard stopper knot in rope work and macramé. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stopper knot formed by looping a cord into a figure-8 shape before threading the end back through the first loop. It is bulkier than an overhand knot and easier to undo after loading.
Any knot tied at the end of a cord to prevent it passing through a hole or back through a knot group. The figure-8 and the overhand knot are the two most-used stopper knots in macramé.

content.text: with a lower profile than the overhand knot and easier to untie after it has been under load. It is larger than an overhand knot on the same cord diameter, so it is the better choice where the cord needs to grip firmly against a hole or a ring.

steps: Hold the cord with the working end to the right. Form a loop by bringing the working end over the standing part, creating a simple overhand loop.
Pass the working end under and around the standing part, moving to the left — this creates the cross bar of the figure-8.
Bring the working end back to the right and pass it down through the first loop from top to bottom.
Pull the working end and the standing part in opposite directions to tighten. The knot should show the figure-8 shape clearly before it closes up.

content.text: The overhand knot is smaller and faster to tie, but under load it tightens to a hard lump that is difficult to undo. The figure-8 knot is slightly larger and can almost always be loosened by rolling it between finger and thumb even after sustained tension. Use an overhand knot where size is important; use a figure-8 where the piece may need to be adjusted or disassembled later.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: One common error when tying figure-8 knots.

items.symptom: Knot capsizes into an overhand knot shape when tightened
items.cause: The working end was passed through the loop from the wrong direction (bottom to top instead of top to bottom).
items.fix: The working end must enter the first loop from above and exit below. Re-tie from step 1. If it looks like an overhand when tightened, it has capsized.
